title: VN — Value Network: Steam and Air-Conditioning Supply (NAICS 221330)

sections.title: Header
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS Code** | 221330 — Steam and Air-Conditioning Supply |
| **CFJ** | Generate, distribute, and meter thermal energy (heating and cooling) from centralised plants to multiple buildings via closed-pipe networks |
| **Output Types** | District hot-water heating; District steam heating; District chilled-water cooling; Combined heating & cooling (4-pipe); Campus/industrial thermal utility |
| **Hierarchy** | Process VN (L7 through L4) — what process steps, equipment, and subsystems compose district energy generation, distribution, and consumption metering |
| **Key Operators** | Vattenfall, ENGIE, Fortum, Veolia, Statkraft (network operators); Danfoss, Kamstrup, Siemens, Diehl, Landis+Gyr (metering/controls) [SRC: web_search] |
| **Market Size** | ~$187–214B globally (district heating, 2024); heat meter market projected to reach $3.24B by 2035; EU EED mandates individual metering at every building connection [SRC: web_search] |
| **Architecture Distance** | 3 (adjacent — same media, pipe sizes, and flow+temperature measurement need, but utility-scale not OEM-embedded) [SRC: 05_market_discovery] |

---

sections.title: L7 — Ecosystem
sections.content:
### L7: District Energy Ecosystem

The complete ecosystem of thermal energy utilities, equipment OEMs, pipe manufacturers, metering companies, building operators, and regulators coordinated around centralised generation and piped distribution of heating and cooling to multiple buildings. Encompasses district hot-water networks, district steam networks, district chilled-water networks, and combined heating/cooling systems serving residential, commercial, and industrial consumers. The global district heating market was valued at ~$187–214B in 2024 with a CAGR of ~4.6%, driven by decarbonisation mandates, waste heat recovery, and the EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED 2012/27/EU) requiring individual consumption metering at every building connection. NAICS 221330 in the U.S. comprises ~41 active establishments; the industry is far larger in Northern Europe where district heating serves >50% of buildings in Denmark, Finland, and Sweden. [SRC: web_search, Census Bureau]

---

sections.title: L6 — System-of-Systems Decomposition
sections.content:
Six L6 domains follow the thermal energy path from centralised generation through distribution network to building-level consumption metering, plus enabling domains.

| L6 ID | Name | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L6.1** | Thermal Energy Generation | Core | Centralised heat/cold sources: CHP plants, hot-water boilers, heat pumps, waste-to-energy, geothermal, biomass, electric chillers, absorption chillers. |
| **L6.2** | Distribution Network | Core | Pre-insulated pipe network, pumping stations, and hydraulic infrastructure transporting thermal energy from central plant to building substations. |
| **L6.3** | Building Substation & Energy Transfer | Core | Transfer station at each building connection: heat exchangers, control valves, differential pressure controllers, and flow/temperature measurement. **Marquardt sits here.** |
| **L6.4** | Metering & Billing | Core | Thermal energy meters (heat meters / BTU meters) measuring delivered energy for consumption-based billing per EU EED / MID MI-004. |
| **L6.5** | Network Control & Optimisation | Horizontal | SCADA, hydraulic balancing, demand forecasting, variable-flow control, weather compensation across the entire network. |
| **L6.6** | Maintenance, Safety & Compliance | Horizontal | Leak detection, pipe integrity, regulatory compliance (pressure equipment directive), water treatment, and emergency response. |

---

sections.title: L6.1 — Thermal Energy Generation
sections.content:
Centralised plant converting primary energy (fuel, waste heat, ambient heat, solar) into thermal energy carried by water or steam. Plant type defines the district system's economics and carbon footprint.

### L5 Systems under L6.1

#### L5.1.1: CHP Plant (Combined Heat and Power)

Gas turbine or reciprocating engine generating electricity with waste heat captured for district heating. Dominant generation source in European networks. Electrical efficiency 35–45%; total efficiency 80–90%. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.1.1: Gas Turbine / Reciprocating Engine | Prime mover burning natural gas, biogas, or hydrogen; 1–50 MW thermal per unit |
| L4.1.1.2: Waste Heat Recovery Boiler | Heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) or exhaust-gas heat exchanger capturing flue gas energy |
| L4.1.1.3: Steam Turbine / ORC Module | Back-pressure or extraction steam turbine; ORC for lower-temperature waste heat |

#### L5.1.2: Hot-Water Boiler Plant

Large-scale hot-water boilers (gas, oil, biomass, electric) for peak-load and backup generation. Typical supply temperatures 80–120 °C (3rd generation) or 50–70 °C (4th generation low-temperature networks). [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.2.1: Industrial Burner Assembly | Modulating gas/oil/biomass burner; 1–100 MW thermal capacity per unit |
| L4.1.2.2: Fire-Tube or Water-Tube Boiler | Pressurised boiler vessel rated to 6–16 bar; steel or cast-iron construction |
| L4.1.2.3: Flue Gas Economiser | Condensing economiser recovering latent heat from exhaust; boosts efficiency 5–10% |

#### L5.1.3: Large-Scale Heat Pump

Electric heat pump extracting thermal energy from ambient sources (seawater, wastewater, ground) for low-temperature district heating. Growing rapidly in Scandinavian networks. COP 3–5 typical. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.3.1: Compressor Unit | Centrifugal or screw compressor; 1–30 MW thermal per unit |
| L4.1.3.2: Evaporator (Source-Side HX) | Plate or shell-and-tube heat exchanger extracting heat from water/wastewater source |
| L4.1.3.3: Condenser (Network-Side HX) | Transfers heat to district heating supply water at 60–90 °C |

#### L5.1.4: Chiller Plant (District Cooling)

Centralised electric or absorption chillers producing chilled water (5–7 °C supply) for district cooling networks. Electric chillers hold ~47% market share in district cooling. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.4.1: Electric Centrifugal Chiller | Variable-speed centrifugal compressor; 500–5,000 TR per unit |
| L4.1.4.2: Absorption Chiller | Lithium bromide or ammonia absorption cycle; driven by waste heat or steam |
| L4.1.4.3: Cooling Tower / Heat Rejection | Open or closed-circuit cooling tower rejecting condenser heat to atmosphere |

#### L5.1.5: Thermal Energy Storage (TES)

Centralised storage buffering generation/demand mismatch. Hot-water tanks (2,000–50,000 m3) for heating; ice or chilled-water tanks for cooling. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.5.1: Stratified Hot-Water Tank | Insulated steel tank with thermocline stratification; charges during low demand |
| L4.1.5.2: Ice Storage / Chilled-Water Tank | Generates and stores ice or chilled water during off-peak hours for peak shaving |
| L4.1.5.3: Tank Instrumentation | Temperature stratification sensors, level gauges, charge/discharge controllers |

#### L5.1.6: Waste Heat Recovery

Captures industrial waste heat (data centres, wastewater treatment, industrial processes) for injection into district heating network. Zero-fuel marginal cost. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.6.1: Waste Heat Exchanger | Plate or shell-and-tube HX at industrial source; typically 40–80 °C |
| L4.1.6.2: Booster Heat Pump | Raises waste-heat temperature to network supply temperature if source is too low |
| L4.1.6.3: Interconnection Piping & Valves | Dedicated pipe spur connecting industrial source to district network trunk |

---

sections.title: L6.2 — Distribution Network
sections.content:
The pipe-side infrastructure transporting thermal energy from central plant(s) to building substations and back. Network length ranges from <1 km (campus) to >1,000 km (city-wide). The pipe network is typically the largest single capital expenditure in a district energy system. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.2

#### L5.2.1: Pre-Insulated Pipe Network

Bonded pre-insulated pipe (steel carrier pipe + PU foam insulation + PE jacket) buried underground. Supply and return pipes run in parallel. Key manufacturers: Logstor, Isoplus, Brugg, Permapipe. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.1.1: Steel Carrier Pipe | Carbon steel pipe (DN50–DN1200) carrying heated/chilled water; welded joints |
| L4.2.1.2: PU Foam Insulation | Rigid polyurethane foam bonded to carrier and jacket; thermal conductivity <0.03 W/mK |
| L4.2.1.3: PE Outer Jacket | High-density polyethylene casing protecting insulation from ground moisture |
| L4.2.1.4: Leak Detection Wire | Copper wire embedded in insulation; impedance monitoring detects moisture ingress |

#### L5.2.2: Network Pumping Station

Centralised and distributed pumps maintaining hydraulic pressure and flow across the network. Variable-speed drives dominate modern networks for energy-efficient flow control.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.2.1: Variable-Speed Circulation Pump | Centrifugal pump with VFD; 10–500 kW per unit; differential pressure controlled |
| L4.2.2.2: Pump Control & VFD | Variable frequency drive with PID control responding to network pressure setpoint |
| L4.2.2.3: Check Valve & Isolation Valve | Non-return valve preventing backflow; gate/butterfly valve for maintenance isolation |

#### L5.2.3: Network Expansion & Pressure Management

Compensates for thermal expansion of carrier pipes and maintains system pressure within design limits.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.3.1: Expansion Compensator | Axial or lateral expansion bellows absorbing pipe thermal expansion; stainless steel |
| L4.2.3.2: Pressurisation Unit | Pump-controlled pressurisation maintaining static pressure; includes expansion vessel |
| L4.2.3.3: Safety Relief Valve | Spring-loaded relief discharging on overpressure; sized per pressure equipment directive |

#### L5.2.4: Network Valves & Fittings

Branch isolation, sectionalising, and flow regulation across the distribution network.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.4.1: Sectionalising Valve | Butterfly or gate valve isolating network sections for maintenance without full shutdown |
| L4.2.4.2: Branch Connection Tee | Welded tee fitting connecting trunk to branch line; insulated to trunk standard |
| L4.2.4.3: Manhole / Valve Chamber | Underground access chamber housing valves, compensators, and instrumentation |

---

sections.title: L6.3 — Building Substation & Energy Transfer
sections.content:
The interface between the district network (primary side) and the building's internal heating/cooling system (secondary side). Every building connection has a substation. The substation is where Marquardt's flow sensor has its primary entry point — flow + temperature measurement on the primary and/or secondary side feeds both the control loop and the energy meter. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.3

#### L5.3.1: Heat Exchanger (Substation)

Plate heat exchanger hydraulically separating primary (network) from secondary (building) circuits. Prevents pressure and water-quality cross-contamination. Brazed or gasketed plate design; 50–2,000 kW typical per building. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.1.1: Brazed/Gasketed Plate Heat Exchanger | Stainless steel plate pack; primary/secondary hydraulic separation; counter-flow design |
| L4.3.1.2: Strainer / Filter | Mesh strainer on primary inlet protecting HX from particles; typically 0.5–1.0 mm mesh |
| L4.3.1.3: Isolation Valves (Primary Side) | Ball or butterfly valves for primary-side shut-off during maintenance |

#### L5.3.2: Substation Control Valve Assembly

Motorised control valve regulating primary-side flow to maintain secondary supply temperature setpoint. The control valve is the primary actuator; the flow sensor provides feedback for the energy calculation. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.2.1: Motorised Control Valve | 2-way or 3-way valve with electric actuator; modulating or on/off; DN20–DN100 |
| L4.3.2.2: Differential Pressure Controller | Self-acting or electronic DP controller maintaining constant delta-P across substation |
| L4.3.2.3: Electronic Controller | PLC or dedicated substation controller reading temperature sensors and modulating valve |

#### L5.3.3: Substation Flow & Temperature Measurement

Instruments measuring volumetric flow rate and supply/return temperatures on both primary and secondary sides. The flow signal and paired temperature signals feed the energy calculation unit for billing-grade thermal energy measurement. This is the primary integration point for Marquardt's ultrasonic flow sensor in district energy. [SRC: web_search]

**Marquardt's ultrasonic flow sensor sits at L4 within this L5 node.**

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| **L4.3.3.1: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor** | **Transit-time ultrasonic measurement; no moving parts, zero pressure drop, glycol-tolerant, integrated temperature sensing. Marquardt DN20/DN25 matches typical substation pipe sizes. Growing adoption replacing mechanical meters.** |
| L4.3.3.2: Mechanical Impeller / Turbine Flow Meter | Single-jet or multi-jet impeller meter; incumbent in legacy substations. Moving parts wear, requiring replacement every 5–6 years. Declining share. |
| L4.3.3.3: Electromagnetic Flow Meter | Mag-flow sensor for conductive liquids; higher accuracy but 3–5x cost of ultrasonic. Used in large commercial/industrial substations. |
| L4.3.3.4: Matched Temperature Sensor Pair | Pt500 or Pt1000 RTD sensors on supply and return; matched to <0.02 K error per EN 1434. Essential for delta-T energy calculation. |

#### L5.3.4: Secondary-Side Distribution

Building-internal hydronic distribution fed by the substation's secondary circuit.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.4.1: Secondary Circulator Pump | Building-side circulator distributing heated/chilled water to risers and zones |
| L4.3.4.2: Mixing / Injection Valve | Blends supply and return water for zone-specific temperature control |
| L4.3.4.3: Building Riser Piping | Vertical distribution piping from substation to floors; steel, copper, or composite |

---

sections.title: L6.4 — Metering & Billing
sections.content:
Certified thermal energy measurement for consumption-based billing. EU EED mandates individual heat metering at every building connection in district heating. EN 1434 and MID MI-004 define accuracy classes and certification requirements. Heat meter = flow sensor + matched temperature pair + integrator/calculator. The heat meter market is projected to reach $3.24B by 2035. Key manufacturers: Kamstrup, Danfoss, Siemens, Diehl, Landis+Gyr. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.4

#### L5.4.1: Heat Meter / Thermal Energy Meter

MID-certified instrument integrating flow × density × Cp × delta-T over time to calculate delivered kWh or MWh. Ultrasonic meters dominate new installations. Kamstrup MULTICAL, Danfoss SonoMeter, Siemens UH50 are market leaders. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.1.1: Flow Sensor (Metering-Grade) | Ultrasonic or mechanical flow sensor certified to EN 1434; accuracy class 2 or better |
| L4.4.1.2: Temperature Sensor Pair (Metering-Grade) | Pt500/Pt1000 matched pair; certified together with flow sensor and calculator |
| L4.4.1.3: Energy Calculator / Integrator | Microprocessor integrating thermal power over time; stores cumulative kWh/MWh; tamper-sealed |

#### L5.4.2: Cooling Meter

Similar to heat meter but measuring extracted cooling energy. Reverse delta-T calculation (return warmer than supply). Growing with district cooling adoption.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.2.1: Flow Sensor (Cooling-Grade) | Same ultrasonic/mechanical sensor; calibrated for chilled-water temperature range (4–15 °C) |
| L4.4.2.2: Temperature Sensor Pair (Cooling-Grade) | Pt500/Pt1000 matched pair optimised for narrow cooling delta-T (3–8 K) |
| L4.4.2.3: Cooling Energy Calculator | Firmware variant calculating cooling energy from inverted delta-T |

#### L5.4.3: Meter Data Communication

Remote reading, data collection, and billing system integration. AMR (automatic meter reading) and AMI (advanced metering infrastructure) enable real-time consumption visibility.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.3.1: Radio / Wireless Module | Wireless M-Bus, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, or proprietary RF for remote meter reading |
| L4.4.3.2: Data Concentrator / Gateway | Collects readings from multiple meters; forwards to head-end system via IP |
| L4.4.3.3: Billing & MDM Software | Meter data management (MDM) and billing system integration; allocates costs per building |

---

sections.title: L6.5 — Network Control & Optimisation (H)
sections.content:
Horizontal domain spanning all core process steps. SCADA, hydraulic modelling, demand forecasting, and variable-flow control optimising the entire network. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.5

#### L5.5.1: SCADA & Network Monitoring

Centralised supervisory control and data acquisition system monitoring plant, network, and substations.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.1.1: SCADA Server & HMI | Industrial PC running real-time monitoring, alarm management, and operator display |
| L4.5.1.2: RTU / PLC at Substation | Remote terminal unit or PLC at each substation relaying sensor data and executing control |
| L4.5.1.3: Communication Network | Fibre optic, cellular (4G/5G), or Ethernet backbone connecting substations to control centre |

#### L5.5.2: Hydraulic Balancing & Optimisation

Algorithms and actuators maintaining balanced flow distribution across the network to minimise pump energy and ensure adequate supply to all buildings.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.2.1: Network Simulation Model | Digital twin / hydraulic simulation software (e.g., Termis, Comsof Heat) for planning and real-time optimisation |
| L4.5.2.2: Variable-Flow Control Logic | Demand-based pump speed and valve modulation reducing return temperature and pump energy |
| L4.5.2.3: Weather Compensation & Forecasting | Outdoor temperature forecast driving supply temperature setpoint curve |

---

sections.title: L6.6 — Maintenance, Safety & Compliance (H)
sections.content:
Horizontal domain ensuring network integrity, water quality, and regulatory compliance across all process steps.

### L5 Systems under L6.6

#### L5.6.1: Leak Detection & Pipe Integrity

Monitoring buried pipe network for insulation degradation and carrier pipe leaks.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.1.1: Wire-Based Leak Detection | Impedance monitoring of copper wires embedded in pipe insulation; detects moisture ingress |
| L4.6.1.2: Thermal Imaging / Aerial Survey | Drone or helicopter thermal survey identifying surface hot spots above buried pipes |
| L4.6.1.3: Acoustic Leak Detection | Listening equipment detecting pressurised water escaping through pipe defects |

#### L5.6.2: Water Treatment & Corrosion Protection

Maintaining water chemistry in the closed network to prevent corrosion, scaling, and biological growth.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.2.1: Chemical Dosing System | Automated dosing of corrosion inhibitor, oxygen scavenger, and biocide |
| L4.6.2.2: Side-Stream Filtration | Bypass filter removing suspended solids and magnetite from network water |
| L4.6.2.3: Water Quality Monitoring | pH, conductivity, dissolved oxygen, and hardness sensors with logging |

#### L5.6.3: Regulatory Compliance & Safety

Pressure equipment directive, building safety codes, environmental permits, and metering calibration.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.3.1: Pressure Equipment Inspection | Periodic inspection of boilers, vessels, and piping per PED / ASME |
| L4.6.3.2: Meter Verification & Re-Calibration | Periodic accuracy verification of heat meters per EN 1434 requirements |
| L4.6.3.3: Environmental Monitoring & Reporting | Emissions monitoring, noise compliance, and sustainability reporting |

---

sections.content:
| L6 Domain | DH Hot Water | DH Steam | DC Chilled Water | Combined H&C | Campus Utility |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| L6.1 Thermal Energy Generation | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y |
| L6.2 Distribution Network | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y |
| L6.3 Building Substation | Y | Y (condensate) | Y | Y | Y |
| L6.4 Metering & Billing | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y |
| L6.5 Network Control (H) | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y |
| L6.6 Maintenance & Safety (H) | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y |

Notes: All L6 domains are activated by all output types — district energy is infrastructure-universal. The differences between output types lie at L5 level (e.g., chillers only for DC; boilers only for DH; steam traps only for steam networks). L6.3 and L6.4 are where flow measurement is most critical — every building connection requires metered energy delivery.

sections.title: Marquardt Position in the Value Network
sections.content:
```
L7   District Energy Ecosystem
 |
 +-- L6.3  Building Substation & Energy Transfer
      |
      +-- L5.3.3  Substation Flow & Temperature Measurement
           |
           +-- L4.3.3.1  Ultrasonic Flow Sensor  <-- MARQUARDT
           +-- L4.3.3.2  Mechanical Impeller / Turbine Flow Meter (incumbent, declining)
           +-- L4.3.3.3  Electromagnetic Flow Meter (premium, large substations)
           +-- L4.3.3.4  Matched Temperature Sensor Pair (complement, not competitor)
```

**Full L-path:** L7 > L6.3 Building Substation & Energy Transfer > L5.3.3 Substation Flow & Temperature Measurement > L4.3.3.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor

**Secondary position:** L6.4 Metering & Billing > L5.4.1 Heat Meter > L4.4.1.1 Flow Sensor (Metering-Grade) — Marquardt's sensor could serve as the flow sensing element inside a heat meter, but this requires EN 1434 / MID certification which is not yet confirmed for the Marquardt sensor.

**Why this market:** District energy operators must measure flow + temperature at every building connection to calculate thermal energy consumption (Q = flow x Cp x delta-T) for billing. The EU Energy Efficiency Directive (2012/27/EU) mandates individual metering. This creates a regulatory-driven, non-discretionary demand for flow sensors at every building connection — potentially thousands per city network. Marquardt's combined flow + temperature sensor in a single body directly matches the substation measurement need. The DN20/DN25 pipe sizes match typical residential substation connections. The zero-pressure-drop and no-moving-parts advantages reduce lifecycle cost vs incumbent mechanical meters that require replacement every 5–6 years. The key barrier is MID/EN 1434 metering certification — without it, Marquardt can serve the substation control loop but not the billing meter. [SRC: 05_market_discovery, web_search]

---

sections.title: Structural Validation Summary
sections.content:
| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| Exactly one L7 node | PASS — 1 L7 (District Energy Ecosystem) |
| L6 count in range (3–8) | PASS — 6 L6 nodes |
| L5 count per L6 in range (2–12) | PASS — L6.1: 6, L6.2: 4, L6.3: 4, L6.4: 3, L6.5: 2, L6.6: 3 |
| L4 count per L5 >= 2 | PASS — all L5 nodes have 3 L4 children |
| No orphan nodes | PASS — every node has a parent |
| No level skipping | PASS — L7>L6>L5>L4 strictly followed |
| Valid parent-child transitions | PASS |
| No company names in L7/L6 names | PASS |
| Three-question naming test (5 spot-checks) | PASS — "Pre-Insulated Pipe Network," "Heat Exchanger," "Pressurisation Unit," "Energy Calculator," "Chemical Dosing System" all industry-standard |
| Unit count in range (15–60) | PASS — 22 L5 + 66 L4 = 89 total units (includes L7 + L6) |

rows.Value: Generate, distribute, and meter thermal energy (heating and cooling) from centralised plants to multiple buildings via closed-pipe networks
District hot-water heating; District steam heating; District chilled-water cooling; Combined heating & cooling (4-pipe); Campus/industrial thermal utility
Process VN (L7 through L4) — what process steps, equipment, and subsystems compose district energy generation, distribution, and consumption metering
Vattenfall, ENGIE, Fortum, Veolia, Statkraft (network operators); Danfoss, Kamstrup, Siemens, Diehl, Landis+Gyr (metering/controls) [SRC: web_search]
~$187–214B globally (district heating, 2024); heat meter market projected to reach $3.24B by 2035; EU EED mandates individual metering at every building connection [SRC: web_search]
3 (adjacent — same media, pipe sizes, and flow+temperature measurement need, but utility-scale not OEM-embedded) [SRC: 05_market_discovery]

rows.Name: Thermal Energy Generation
rows.Description: Centralised heat/cold sources: CHP plants, hot-water boilers, heat pumps, waste-to-energy, geothermal, biomass, electric chillers, absorption chillers.

rows.Name: Distribution Network
rows.Description: Pre-insulated pipe network, pumping stations, and hydraulic infrastructure transporting thermal energy from central plant to building substations.

rows.Name: Building Substation & Energy Transfer
rows.Description: Transfer station at each building connection: heat exchangers, control valves, differential pressure controllers, and flow/temperature measurement. **Marquardt sits here.**

rows.Name: Metering & Billing
rows.Description: Thermal energy meters (heat meters / BTU meters) measuring delivered energy for consumption-based billing per EU EED / MID MI-004.

rows.Name: Network Control & Optimisation
rows.Description: SCADA, hydraulic balancing, demand forecasting, variable-flow control, weather compensation across the entire network.

rows.Name: Maintenance, Safety & Compliance
rows.Description: Leak detection, pipe integrity, regulatory compliance (pressure equipment directive), water treatment, and emergency response.

**Transit-time ultrasonic measurement; no moving parts, zero pressure drop, glycol-tolerant, integrated temperature sensing. Marquardt DN20/DN25 matches typical substation pipe sizes. Growing adoption replacing mechanical meters.**

rows.L4 Subsystem: L4.3.3.2: Mechanical Impeller / Turbine Flow Meter
rows.Description: Single-jet or multi-jet impeller meter; incumbent in legacy substations. Moving parts wear, requiring replacement every 5–6 years. Declining share.

entities.cfj_statement: Generate, distribute, and meter thermal energy (heating and cooling) from centralised plants to multiple buildings via closed-pipe networks

value_network.cfj_statement: Generate, distribute, and meter thermal energy (heating and cooling) from centralised plants to multiple buildings via closed-pipe networks

units.name: District Energy Ecosystem
units.description: Complete ecosystem of thermal energy utilities, equipment OEMs, pipe manufacturers, metering companies, building operators, and regulators coordinated around centralised generation and piped distribution of heating and cooling

units.name: Thermal Energy Generation
units.description: Centralised heat/cold sources: CHP, boilers, heat pumps, waste-to-energy, geothermal, biomass, chillers

units.name: Distribution Network
units.description: Pre-insulated pipe network, pumping stations, and hydraulic infrastructure transporting thermal energy from central plant to building substations

units.name: Building Substation & Energy Transfer
units.description: Transfer station at each building: heat exchangers, control valves, differential pressure controllers, flow/temperature measurement

units.name: Metering & Billing
units.description: Certified thermal energy measurement for consumption-based billing per EU EED / MID MI-004 / EN 1434

units.name: Network Control & Optimisation
units.description: SCADA, hydraulic balancing, demand forecasting, variable-flow control across the entire network

units.name: Maintenance, Safety & Compliance
units.description: Leak detection, pipe integrity, water treatment, regulatory compliance, and emergency response

units.name: CHP Plant
units.description: Combined heat and power plant generating electricity with waste heat for district heating; 80-90% total efficiency

units.name: Hot-Water Boiler Plant
units.description: Large-scale hot-water boilers (gas, oil, biomass, electric) for peak-load and backup; 80-120 C supply

units.name: Large-Scale Heat Pump
units.description: Electric heat pump extracting thermal energy from ambient sources; COP 3-5; growing in Scandinavian networks

units.name: Chiller Plant
units.description: Centralised electric or absorption chillers producing chilled water (5-7 C) for district cooling

units.name: Thermal Energy Storage
units.description: Hot-water tanks or ice storage buffering generation/demand mismatch; 2,000-50,000 m3

units.name: Waste Heat Recovery
units.description: Captures industrial waste heat (data centres, wastewater, industry) for network injection; zero-fuel cost

units.name: Pre-Insulated Pipe Network
units.description: Bonded pre-insulated pipe (steel + PU foam + PE jacket) buried underground; supply and return in parallel

units.name: Network Pumping Station
units.description: Centralised and distributed pumps maintaining hydraulic pressure and flow; variable-speed drives

units.name: Network Expansion & Pressure Management
units.description: Compensates thermal expansion and maintains system pressure within design limits

units.name: Network Valves & Fittings
units.description: Branch isolation, sectionalising, and flow regulation across the distribution network

units.name: Heat Exchanger (Substation)
units.description: Plate HX separating primary (network) from secondary (building) circuits; 50-2,000 kW per building

units.name: Substation Control Valve Assembly
units.description: Motorised control valve regulating primary-side flow to maintain secondary supply temperature

units.name: Substation Flow & Temperature Measurement
units.description: Flow and temperature instruments feeding energy calculation and control loop; MARQUARDT POSITION

units.name: Secondary-Side Distribution
units.description: Building-internal hydronic distribution fed by the substation secondary circuit

units.name: Heat Meter
units.description: MID-certified instrument: flow sensor + matched temp pair + integrator calculating delivered kWh/MWh

units.name: Cooling Meter
units.description: Thermal energy meter for district cooling; reverse delta-T calculation

units.name: Meter Data Communication
units.description: Remote reading (AMR/AMI), data collection, and billing system integration

units.name: SCADA & Network Monitoring
units.description: Centralised supervisory control and data acquisition monitoring plant, network, and substations

units.name: Hydraulic Balancing & Optimisation
units.description: Algorithms and actuators maintaining balanced flow distribution across the network

units.name: Leak Detection & Pipe Integrity
units.description: Monitoring buried pipe network for insulation degradation and carrier pipe leaks

units.name: Water Treatment & Corrosion Protection
units.description: Maintaining water chemistry to prevent corrosion, scaling, and biological growth

units.name: Regulatory Compliance & Safety
units.description: Pressure equipment inspection, meter verification, environmental monitoring

units.name: Gas Turbine / Reciprocating Engine
units.description: Prime mover burning natural gas, biogas, or hydrogen; 1-50 MW thermal per unit

units.name: Waste Heat Recovery Boiler
units.description: HRSG or exhaust-gas heat exchanger capturing flue gas energy

units.name: Steam Turbine / ORC Module
units.description: Back-pressure or extraction steam turbine; ORC for lower-temperature waste heat

units.name: Industrial Burner Assembly
units.description: Modulating gas/oil/biomass burner; 1-100 MW thermal capacity per unit

units.name: Fire-Tube or Water-Tube Boiler
units.description: Pressurised boiler vessel rated to 6-16 bar; steel or cast-iron construction

units.name: Flue Gas Economiser
units.description: Condensing economiser recovering latent heat from exhaust; 5-10% efficiency boost

units.name: Compressor Unit
units.description: Centrifugal or screw compressor; 1-30 MW thermal per unit

units.name: Evaporator (Source-Side HX)
units.description: Plate or shell-and-tube HX extracting heat from water/wastewater source

units.name: Condenser (Network-Side HX)
units.description: Transfers heat to district heating supply water at 60-90 C

units.name: Electric Centrifugal Chiller
units.description: Variable-speed centrifugal compressor; 500-5,000 TR per unit

units.name: Absorption Chiller
units.description: Lithium bromide or ammonia absorption cycle; driven by waste heat or steam

units.name: Cooling Tower / Heat Rejection
units.description: Open or closed-circuit cooling tower rejecting condenser heat to atmosphere

units.name: Stratified Hot-Water Tank
units.description: Insulated steel tank with thermocline stratification; charges during low demand

units.name: Ice Storage / Chilled-Water Tank
units.description: Generates and stores ice or chilled water during off-peak hours for peak shaving

units.name: Tank Instrumentation
units.description: Temperature stratification sensors, level gauges, charge/discharge controllers

units.name: Waste Heat Exchanger
units.description: Plate or shell-and-tube HX at industrial source; typically 40-80 C

units.name: Booster Heat Pump
units.description: Raises waste-heat temperature to network supply temperature if source is too low

units.name: Interconnection Piping & Valves
units.description: Dedicated pipe spur connecting industrial source to district network trunk

units.name: Steel Carrier Pipe
units.description: Carbon steel pipe DN50-DN1200 carrying heated/chilled water; welded joints

units.name: PU Foam Insulation
units.description: Rigid polyurethane foam bonded to carrier and jacket; <0.03 W/mK

units.name: PE Outer Jacket
units.description: High-density polyethylene casing protecting insulation from ground moisture

units.name: Leak Detection Wire
units.description: Copper wire embedded in insulation; impedance monitoring detects moisture ingress

units.name: Variable-Speed Circulation Pump
units.description: Centrifugal pump with VFD; 10-500 kW per unit; differential pressure controlled

units.name: Pump Control & VFD
units.description: Variable frequency drive with PID control responding to network pressure setpoint

units.name: Check Valve & Isolation Valve
units.description: Non-return valve preventing backflow; gate/butterfly valve for maintenance isolation

units.name: Expansion Compensator
units.description: Axial or lateral expansion bellows absorbing pipe thermal expansion; stainless steel

units.name: Pressurisation Unit
units.description: Pump-controlled pressurisation maintaining static pressure; includes expansion vessel

units.name: Safety Relief Valve
units.description: Spring-loaded relief discharging on overpressure; sized per pressure equipment directive

units.name: Sectionalising Valve
units.description: Butterfly or gate valve isolating network sections for maintenance

units.name: Branch Connection Tee
units.description: Welded tee fitting connecting trunk to branch line; insulated to trunk standard

units.name: Manhole / Valve Chamber
units.description: Underground access chamber housing valves, compensators, and instrumentation

units.name: Brazed/Gasketed Plate Heat Exchanger
units.description: Stainless steel plate pack; primary/secondary hydraulic separation; counter-flow

units.name: Strainer / Filter
units.description: Mesh strainer on primary inlet protecting HX from particles; 0.5-1.0 mm mesh

units.name: Isolation Valves (Primary Side)
units.description: Ball or butterfly valves for primary-side shut-off during maintenance

units.name: Motorised Control Valve
units.description: 2-way or 3-way valve with electric actuator; modulating; DN20-DN100

units.name: Differential Pressure Controller
units.description: Self-acting or electronic DP controller maintaining constant delta-P across substation

units.name: Electronic Controller
units.description: PLC or dedicated substation controller reading sensors and modulating valve

units.name: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor
units.description: Transit-time ultrasonic measurement; no moving parts, zero pressure drop, glycol-tolerant, integrated temperature. MARQUARDT POSITION.

units.name: Mechanical Impeller / Turbine Flow Meter
units.description: Single-jet or multi-jet impeller meter; incumbent in legacy substations; 5-6 year replacement cycle

units.description: Mag-flow sensor for conductive liquids; 3-5x cost of ultrasonic; large substations
